question_structure.context: Two small balls of mass $m$ each with charges $+q$ and $-q$ respectively, are connected by a rigid massless rod of length $d$, forming a dipole. The dipole is parallel to the XY-plane and placed in a uniform magnetic field $\vec{B}$ perpendicular to this plane. Initially, the dipole is aligned with the X-axis and has an initial angular velocity $\omega_0$ in the XY-plane. Its center of mass is at the origin with an initial velocity $\vec{v}_0$ parallel to the XY-plane.
question_structure.sub_question_1: Given $\vec{v}_{0}=0$, find the minimal $\omega_{0}=\omega_{\text{min}}$ necessary for the dipole to reverse its orientation during the motion.
question_structure.sub_question_2: If the dipole starts with $\vec{v}_{0}=0$ and $\omega_{0}=\omega_{\text{min}}$ found in part (c), the trajectory of its center of mass has an asymptote. Find the distance $D$ from the origin to the asymptote.

sub_question_1.step_1: The net force on the dipole is given by $\vec{F}=2 q(\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}) \times \vec{B}=(\vec{\omega} \times \vec{p}) \times \vec{B}$, where $\vec{p}$ is the dipole moment. The time derivative of the dipole moment is $\frac{d \vec{p}}{d t}=\vec{\omega} \times \vec{p}$.
sub_question_1.step_2: Using Newton's second law, $2 m \frac{d \vec{v}}{d t}=\vec{F}=\frac{d \vec{p}}{d t} \times \vec{B}$. Integrating, we obtain a conserved quantity: $2 m \vec{v}-\vec{p} \times \vec{B}=\mathrm{const}$.
sub_question_1.step_3: If $\vec{p}$ reverses direction from $\vec{p}_{0}$ to $\vec{p}_{1}=-\vec{p}_{0}$, then the change in velocity is $\vec{v}_{1}=\vec{v}_{0}+\frac{\left(\vec{p}_{1}-\vec{p}_{0}\right) \times \vec{B}}{2 m}=-\frac{\vec{p}_{0} \times \vec{B}}{m}$.
sub_question_1.step_4: Since the magnetic field does no work, kinetic energy is conserved: $\frac{I}{2} \omega_{0}^{2}=\frac{I}{2} \omega_{1}^{2}+\frac{2 m}{2} v_{1}^{2}$, where $I=\frac{m d^2}{2}$ is the moment of inertia. To minimize $\omega_0$, we set $\omega_1 = 0$.
sub_question_1.step_5: Solving for the minimal angular velocity, we get $\omega_{\min }=v_{1} \sqrt{\frac{2 m}{I}}=\frac{p_{0} B}{m} \sqrt{\frac{4}{d^{2}}}=\frac{2 q B}{m}$.
sub_question_1.step_6: Alternatively, considering the equations of translational motion, we have $\dot{v}_{x}=\dot{\theta} \frac{q B d}{2 m} \cos \theta$ and $\dot{v}_{y}=\dot{\theta} \frac{q B d}{2 m} \sin \theta$. Integrating these with initial zero velocity, $v_{x}=\frac{q B d}{2 m} \sin \theta$, and  $v_{y}=\frac{q B d}{2 m}(1-\cos \theta)$.
sub_question_1.step_7: Using the torque expression, the equation of rotational motion is given by $\ddot{\theta}=-\frac{q^{2} B^{2} d^{2}}{2 m} \sin \theta$. This simplifies to $\ddot{\theta}+\frac{q^{2} B^{2}}{m^{2}} \sin \theta=0$, analogous to a pendulum.
sub_question_1.step_8: Equating initial kinetic energy to potential energy when reaching the top, $\frac{1}{2} m L^2 \dot{\theta_0}^2 = 2 m g L$. Therefore, $\omega_{\min }=\dot{\theta}_{0}=\sqrt{4 \frac{g}{L}}=2 \frac{q B}{m}$.

sub_question_2.step_9: If the dipole's trajectory has an asymptote, motion along it is uniform, implying $\omega=0$ and that the dipole reversed its orientation. The velocity on the asymptote is $\vec{v}_{1}=p_{0} B \hat{\jmath} / m$.  The asymptote will be parallel to the Y-axis, with an equation of the form $x=D$.
sub_question_2.step_10: The angular momentum around the origin $L_O$ is $\frac{d \vec{L}_{O}}{d t}=\vec{R}_{+} \times\left(q \dot{\vec{R}}_{+} \times \vec{B}\right)+\vec{R}_{-} \times\left(-q \dot{\vec{R}}_{-} \times \vec{B}\right)= -\frac{q \vec{B}}{2} \frac{d}{d t}\left(R_{+}^{2}-R_{-}^{2}\right)$.
sub_question_2.step_11: Integration yields the conserved quantity: $\vec{L}_{O}+\frac{q \vec{B}}{2}\left(R_{+}^{2}-R_{-}^{2}\right)=\vec{L}_{O}+\vec{B}(\vec{R} \cdot \vec{p})=\mathrm{const}$, where $\vec{R}$ is the center of mass position.
sub_question_2.step_12: Initially, $L_{O}(0)=I \omega_{0}=q B d^{2}$. At the asymptote, $L_{O}(\infty)+ B\left(\vec{R}_{1} \cdot \vec{p}_{1}\right)=2 m D \frac{p_{0} B}{m}-B D p_{0}=B D q d$.
sub_question_2.step_13: Equating the initial and final angular momentum gives $q B d^{2} =  B D q d$, hence $D=d$.
sub_question_2.step_14: Alternatively, the distance to the asymptote is $D=x_{\infty}=\int_{0}^{\infty} v_{x} d t=\frac{q B d}{2 m} \int_{0}^{\infty} \sin \theta d t$.
sub_question_2.step_15: From equation of rotational motion, we have $\int_{0}^{\infty} \sin \theta d t = -\frac{m^{2}}{q^{2} B^{2}} \int_{0}^{\infty} \ddot{\theta} d t=  -\frac{m^{2}}{q^{2} B^{2}}\left(\dot{\theta}_{1}-\dot{\theta}_{0}\right)=\frac{m^{2}}{q^{2} B^{2}} \omega_{\min }=\frac{2 m}{q B}$.
sub_question_2.step_16: Substituting this into the expression for D we have $D=\frac{q B d}{2 m} \frac{2 m}{q B}=d$.

image_captions: The image depicts a system in the XY plane. A rod of length d lies along the x-axis, with a particle of charge -q and mass m at the left end and a particle of charge +q and mass m at the right end. The rod is rotating counterclockwise about its center, with an angular velocity ω₀. A velocity vector v₀ is depicted originating at the center of the rod and pointing upwards to the right. A magnetic field B points out of the page at the top right of the diagram.

step_1.physical_theorem: Lorentz force law and definition of dipole moment

result_quantity.name: Change in velocity when dipole reverses

step_4.physical_theorem: Conservation of kinetic energy and definition of moment of inertia

step_6.physical_theorem: Equations of translational motion and integration

step_7.physical_theorem: Torque expression and equation of rotational motion

step_9.physical_theorem: Asymptotic motion and velocity of dipole

result_quantity.name: Rate of change of angular momentum

step_11.physical_theorem: Integration and conservation of angular momentum

result_quantity.name: Conserved angular momentum quantity
result_quantity.unit: kg m^2/s + T m A m^2

step_12.physical_theorem: Angular momentum at initial and final states

result_quantity.name: Angular momentum at the asymptote
result_quantity.equation: $L_{O}(\infty)+ B\left(\vec{R}_{1} \cdot \vec{p}_{1}\right)=2 m D \frac{p_{0} B}{m}-B D p_{0}=B D q d$
result_quantity.unit: kg m^2/s + T m A m^2

step_13.physical_theorem: Equating initial and final angular momentum

result_quantity.name: Distance to the asymptote
result_quantity.equation: $q B d^{2} =  B D q d$

step_14.physical_theorem: Integral of velocity to get distance

result_quantity.name: Distance to the asymptote
result_quantity.equation: $D=x_{\infty}=\int_{0}^{\infty} v_{x} d t=\frac{q B d}{2 m} \int_{0}^{\infty} \sin \theta d t$

step_15.physical_theorem: Integration of the rotational equation of motion

result_quantity.name: Integral of sin(theta) over time
result_quantity.equation: $\int_{0}^{\infty} \sin \theta d t = -\frac{m^{2}}{q^{2} B^{2}} \int_{0}^{\infty} \ddot{\theta} d t=  -\frac{m^{2}}{q^{2} B^{2}}\left(\dot{\theta}_{1}-\dot{\theta}_{0}\right)=\frac{m^{2}}{q^{2} B^{2}} \omega_{\min }=\frac{2 m}{q B}$
